d.e: the 3rd year of the Jianwen era

v.t: Battle of Baihe River during the Jingnan Campaign
v.s: The Jingnan Campaign of the Ming dynasty entered a critical phase. The Jianwen Emperor sent General Li Jinglong with 600,000 troops to march north. The Yan prince Zhu Di engaged the imperial army in a decisive battle at Baihe River (near present-day Xiong County, Hebei). The Yan forces initially struggled, but Zhu Di personally led elite cavalry to charge the enemy line. A sudden strong wind toppled the imperial army's command flag, causing chaos and a full rout. This victory allowed Zhu Di to shift from defense to offense, laying the foundation for his eventual capture of Nanjing.

v.t: Timurid Empire prepares to invade Ming China
v.s: The Timurid Empire under Timur had built a vast realm from Delhi to Damascus. Timur planned a campaign against Ming China to restore the Mongol Empire. In 1401, he began assembling a large army and stockpiling supplies, while sending spies to gather intelligence on the Ming. However, due to Timur's advanced age and logistical difficulties, the invasion never materialized; Timur died in 1405 during the expedition.

v.t: Ottoman-Timurid tensions rise
v.s: Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I had annexed several Turkic beyliks in Anatolia, bringing his empire into direct contact and conflict with the Timurid Empire. Diplomatic exchanges turned hostile, and war became inevitable. European powers closely watched the looming confrontation, which would decide the fate of Anatolia and the Balkans.

d.e: the 1st year of the Yongle era

v.t: Battle of Ankara – Timur defeats the Ottomans decisively
v.s: On July 20, 1402, Timur led about 140,000 troops to face Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I near Ankara (present-day Turkish capital). Bayezid commanded about 85,000 men, including his elite Janissaries. Timur used superior cavalry mobility, luring the Ottomans from their defensive positions by cutting off water, then encircled and crushed them. Bayezid was captured and died in captivity. The Ottoman Empire fell into an 11-year interregnum as Bayezid's sons fought for the throne, nearly destroying the state. The battle delayed the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople, giving the Byzantine Empire nearly half a century of respite.

v.t: Zhu Di captures Nanjing; Jianwen Emperor disappears
v.s: In June 1402, the Yan prince Zhu Di crossed the Yangtze River and approached Nanjing. The defending general Li Jinglong opened the Jinchuan Gate and surrendered, allowing Zhu Di's army to enter the city. The imperial palace caught fire, and the Jianwen Emperor Zhu Yunwen was never seen again – some say he died in the flames, others that he escaped disguised as a monk. On July 17, Zhu Di ascended the throne with the era name Yongle, becoming the Yongle Emperor. He brutally purged Jianwen's loyalists; Fang Xiaoru, who refused to draft the abdication edict, was executed with his entire extended family. The disappearance of the Jianwen Emperor remains the greatest mystery of the Ming dynasty.

v.t: Beginning of the Ottoman Interregnum
v.s: Bayezid I's four sons – Suleyman, Isa, Mehmed, and Musa – each carved out territories supported by their followers and fought among themselves. Suleyman controlled the European portion (Rumelia), while Mehmed held eastern Anatolia. The Byzantine Empire regained some territory by playing the princes against each other, and various Turkic beyliks in Anatolia reasserted their independence.

d.e: the 1st year of the Yongle era

v.t: Ming plans to move capital to Beijing
v.s: The Yongle Emperor considered Beiping (present-day Beijing) his power base and needed to strengthen northern defenses against remnants of the Northern Yuan. In 1403, he began planning to move the capital. Beiping was renamed Shuntian Prefecture and designated the "temporary capital." Large-scale construction of city walls and palaces commenced, with timber and artisans mobilized from Jiangnan, Huguang, and elsewhere. The formal move took place in 1421, profoundly affecting Ming politics for over two centuries.

v.t: Compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia begins
v.s: To showcase cultural achievements and win over scholars, the Yongle Emperor ordered Xie Jin and other Confucian scholars to compile a vast encyclopedia covering the classics, histories, philosophy, astronomy, geography, and all knowledge. The first draft was submitted in 1404 but dissatisfied the emperor. He appointed Yao Guangxiao to lead a team of 3,000 scholars. Completed in 1407, the Yongle Encyclopedia comprised 22,877 volumes (about 370 million characters), making it the largest general encyclopedia in pre-modern China.

d.e: the 2nd year of the Yongle era

v.t: Timur assembles his invasion force against Ming
v.s: Timur convened a war council in Samarkand and decided to march on Ming China. He mobilized about 200,000 troops, including elite cavalry and infantry from Transoxiana, Persia, and India, plus siege equipment. Timur sent his grandson Khalil Sultan as vanguard toward the Syr Darya River, and he himself set out in January of the following year. However, severe cold and Timur's advanced age (67) hampered the campaign, and he never reached the Ming border.

v.t: Glyndŵr Rising reaches its peak in Wales
v.s: Welsh leader Owain Glyndŵr had been winning victories against the English since 1400. In 1404, he captured several castles, convened a parliament at Machynlleth, proclaimed himself Prince of Wales, and established a rudimentary Welsh government. He also allied with France, which landed troops in 1405. This was the most successful Welsh independence movement of the Middle Ages, though it was eventually suppressed by 1415.

d.e: the 3rd year of the Yongle era

v.t: Timur dies during the march against Ming
v.s: On February 18, 1405, Timur died at Otrar (near present-day Shymkent, Kazakhstan) at age 69 while leading his army against Ming China. His death halted the expedition, and the army returned to Samarkand. The Timurid Empire quickly fell into a succession war; although it nominally survived for decades, it never regained its peak.

v.t: Zheng He's first voyage to the Western Ocean
v.s: On July 11, 1405, the Yongle Emperor ordered the eunuch admiral Zheng He to lead a massive fleet from Nanjing on his first voyage to the Western Ocean. The fleet included over 200 ships and 27,800 men, with the largest treasure ships measuring 44 zhang (about 148 meters) – the largest wooden sailing ships in the world at that time. The fleet visited Champa (Vietnam), Java, Sumatra, and finally Calicut (Kozhikode, India). The main goals were to display Ming power, establish tributary relations, and locate the missing Jianwen Emperor.

v.t: First Ming northern campaign against the Tatars fails
v.s: In 1405, the Yongle Emperor sent Qiu Fu with an army to attack the Tatars, but Qiu Fu was overconfident and his entire force was annihilated. The Yongle Emperor was furious and decided to lead campaigns personally. From 1409 onward, he personally led five expeditions into the steppe to destroy the remnants of the Northern Yuan.

d.e: the 4th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Ming invasion of Annam (Vietnam)
v.s: In 1406, the Yongle Emperor used the pretext that the Trần dynasty had ended and that the usurper Hồ Quý Ly was harassing the border. He ordered the Duke of Cheng, Zhu Neng, and the Marquis of Xincheng, Zhang Fu, to lead a massive army (claimed 800,000) to invade Annam. Earlier, the Trần loyalist Phạm Bạch Hổ and the late king's brother Trần Thiêm Bình had sought Ming help, but Hồ forces ambushed and killed the prince. Ming forces advanced rapidly and captured Hồ Quý Ly and his son the following year. Annam was annexed as Jiaozhi Province.

v.t: Construction of the Forbidden City begins in Beijing
v.s: The Yongle Emperor ordered the formal construction of the imperial palace in Beijing. The plan was modeled on Nanjing but on a larger scale. Architects included Cai Xin and Wu Zhong, mobilizing 300,000 craftsmen and 1 million laborers across the country. Timber came from deep forests in Sichuan, Huguang, and Jiangxi; stone was quarried from Fangshan near Beijing. The Forbidden City was largely completed in 1420, becoming the world's most magnificent palace complex.

d.e: the 5th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Yongle Encyclopedia completed
v.s: The Yongle Encyclopedia was completed after four years under the direction of Yao Guangxiao and Xie Jin, with more than 3,000 scholars involved. It comprised 22,877 chapters plus 60 volumes of table of contents, bound into 11,095 volumes, about 370 million characters, covering classics, histories, philosophy, astronomy, geography, medicine, divination, Buddhism, and Daoism. Only one manuscript copy was made initially; a second copy was made in the Jiajing era. The original was lost by the end of the Ming, and only about 400 volumes of the second copy survive, scattered worldwide.

v.t: Ming establishes Jiaozhi Provincial Administration Commission in Annam
v.s: In June 1407, Ming forces captured Hồ Quý Ly and his son, pacifying Annam. The Yongle Emperor ordered Annam to be reorganized as Jiaozhi Province, divided into 17 prefectures, 47 sub-prefectures, and 157 counties, directly incorporated into the Chinese empire. Ming officials introduced Confucian education, built roads, and fortified cities. However, the Vietnamese people resisted constantly, and Ming rule lasted only about 20 years; it was abandoned in 1427.

v.t: Zheng He's second voyage to the Western Ocean
v.s: In 1407, Zheng He led his fleet on a second voyage, primarily to escort envoys from various countries home. The fleet reached Champa, Java, Siam (Thailand), Sumatra, Ceylon (Sri Lanka), and Calicut. At Ceylon, Zheng He erected a stele inscribed in Chinese, Tamil, and Persian, paying homage to Buddha, Vishnu, and Allah, reflecting a peaceful and inclusive diplomatic philosophy.

d.e: the 6th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Shah Rukh unifies Persia in the Timurid Empire
v.s: Timur's fourth son, Shah Rukh, emerged victorious from the succession wars, controlling Khorasan and eastern Persia. In 1408, he took Samarkand from his nephew Khalil and later defeated other rivals, reunifying the Timurid Empire. Shah Rukh moved the capital to Herat, promoted Islam, and encouraged arts and sciences, making Herat an Islamic cultural center. He maintained friendly relations with Ming China, sending many tribute missions.

v.t: Ming establishes Nurgan Regional Military Commission in the northeast
v.s: The Yongle Emperor established the Nurgan Regional Military Commission at Telin (present-day Tynda, Russia) on the lower Amur River to govern the Jurchen tribes. The Ming sent eunuch Yishiha with fleets to patrol the region, building Yongning Temple and erecting steles. This was an important step in consolidating Ming control over the northeastern frontier, maintaining stability through the appointment of Jurchen chieftains and horse markets.

d.e: the 7th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Yongle Emperor's first personal campaign into the northern steppe
v.s: In 1409, the Yongle Emperor led 500,000 troops on his first personal campaign against the Tatars. The Tatar khan Bunyashiri had killed the Ming envoy Guo Ji and defeated a previous expedition under Qiu Fu. Zhu Di crushed Bunyashiri at the Onon River (Bunyashiri fled and was later killed). He then defeated Arukh's forces at Jinglu. However, supply shortages and intense heat forced him to withdraw, leaving the Mongols not completely destroyed.

v.t: Council of Pisa
v.s: In 1409, the Catholic Church convened the Council of Pisa to resolve the Western Schism (two popes, in Avignon and Rome). The council declared both popes deposed and elected Alexander V as a third pope. However, the original two refused to step down, resulting in three popes simultaneously, worsening the schism. The crisis was finally resolved at the Council of Constance in 1417.

d.e: the 8th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Battle of Grunwald (Battle of Tannenberg)
v.s: On July 15, 1410, a Polish-Lithuanian army of about 39,000 under King Władysław II Jagiełło and Grand Duke Vytautas faced 27,000 elite Teutonic Knights led by Grand Master Ulrich von Jungingen. The Teutonic Knights had heavy cavalry, but the Polish-Lithuanian forces – with the Poles holding the center and the Lithuanians encircling – won a decisive victory. Jungingen and most of the knightly leadership were killed. The battle marked the decline of the Teutonic Order and the rise of Poland-Lithuania as a Central European power.

v.t: Yongle Emperor's second personal campaign into the northern steppe
v.s: In 1410, the Yongle Emperor led his second campaign against Arukh of the Tatars. Ming forces crushed Arukh at Khurkh Khuree (southeast of present-day Ulaanbaatar), and Arukh fled far away. Afterwards, Arukh submitted as a Ming vassal, and the Yongle Emperor created him Prince of Hening. However, the Oirats (Western Mongols) rose rapidly, becoming a new northern threat to the Ming.

d.e: the 9th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Treaty of Thorn
v.s: In February 1411, Poland-Lithuania and the Teutonic Order signed the Treaty of Thorn. The Order paid 100,000 pounds sterling in indemnity and ceded some territories including Danzig. After this defeat, the Order's military and economic strength were severely weakened, and it was forced to make concessions to peasants and townsmen, leading to internal conflicts and terminal decline.

v.t: Zheng He's third voyage to the Western Ocean
v.s: In 1411, Zheng He embarked on his third voyage, reaching the western coast of India, the Persian Gulf, and the East African coast. He brought back large quantities of ivory, spices, and exotic animals; a giraffe (mistaken for a qilin) captured in East Africa was presented to the Yongle Emperor, who considered it an auspicious omen. Through peaceful trade and gifts, Zheng He's fleet persuaded more than 30 Indian Ocean states to send envoys to the Ming court.

d.e: the 10th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Yongle Emperor's third personal campaign into the northern steppe
v.s: In 1412, the Yongle Emperor led his third campaign, this time against the Oirats. Ming forces encountered the Oirat leader Mahamu at the Tuul River. The Ming used their advantage in firearms to repel Oirat cavalry charges, inflicting heavy casualties. Mahamu fled. Afterwards, the Oirats submitted to the Ming, temporarily restoring peace on the northern border.

v.t: Collapse of the Mayapan League
v.s: On the Yucatán Peninsula, the Mayapan League – the main political alliance of the late Maya – collapsed due to internal noble rivalry. Maya city-states fell back into fragmentation and frequent warfare. By the time the Spanish arrived, there were 16 major Maya city-states, many surviving into the 16th century.

d.e: the 11th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Union of Horodło between Poland and Lithuania
v.s: In 1413, Poland and Lithuania signed the Union of Horodło, further strengthening their ties. The treaty provided for joint election of the monarch and granted Lithuanian nobles the same rights and coats of arms as Polish nobles. This solidified cooperation against the Teutonic threat. Poland-Lithuania became a de facto personal union.

v.t: Ming establishes Guizhou Provincial Administration Commission
v.s: In 1413, the Ming officially established Guizhou Province, making it the 13th province. Located in Miao territory, the Ming strengthened control over southwestern chieftains through military conquest and agricultural colonies, laying the foundation for later reforms (gaituguiliu), but also provoking repeated Miao rebellions.

d.e: the 12th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Yongle Emperor's fourth personal campaign into the northern steppe
v.s: In 1414, the Yongle Emperor led his fourth campaign. The Oirat leader Mahamu rebelled again. Ming forces fought the Oirats at Khurkh Khuree, using cannons and handguns to defeat Oirat cavalry, and Mahamu fled northwest. On the return march, the emperor fell ill at Yingchang but recovered.

v.t: Council of Constance opens
v.s: In 1414, the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund convened the Council of Constance to end the three-pope Western Schism. The council attracted tens of thousands of clergy, scholars, and diplomats. It finally deposed the three popes and elected Martin V in 1417, and also condemned Jan Hus as a heretic and had him burned at the stake. It was one of the largest councils in Catholic history.

d.e: the 13th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Battle of Agincourt
v.s: On October 25, 1415, King Henry V of England with about 6,000 men met a French army of 20,000 led by the Count of Albret at Agincourt in northern France. The English, with 4,000 longbowmen, exploited the narrow terrain to neutralize French heavy cavalry. English longbowmen could fire 12 arrows per minute; the French lost 6,000 killed, while English losses were about 400. Henry V then conquered Normandy, and the Treaty of Troyes (1420) made him heir to the French throne. The battle shattered the myth of French chivalry.

v.t: Portuguese conquest of Ceuta
v.s: In August 1415, King John I of Portugal led an army that captured the North African port of Ceuta (present-day Spanish enclave). Located on the southern shore of the Strait of Gibraltar, Ceuta was a hub for African gold and slave trades. The conquest brought great wealth and a trading base, stimulating further exploration down the African coast. Prince Henry the Navigator participated and dedicated himself thereafter to maritime endeavors.

v.t: Jan Hus burned at the stake
v.s: On July 6, 1415, the Czech religious reformer Jan Hus was condemned as a heretic by the Council of Constance and burned at the stake. Hus advocated for vernacular liturgy, lay communion under both kinds, and secularization of church property, and he rejected papal authority. His death sparked the 17-year Hussite Wars in Bohemia. The Hussite movement paved the way for Martin Luther's Reformation in the late 15th century.

d.e: the 14th year of the Yongle era

v.t: End of the Ottoman Interregnum; Mehmed I reunifies the empire
v.s: After more than a decade of civil war, Ottoman prince Mehmed I defeated his brothers Suleyman, Isa, and Musa, and by 1413 had reunified the empire. In 1416, he successfully suppressed the great religious-social rebellion of Sheikh Bedreddin (who demanded common ownership of land and religious equality), consolidating his power. Mehmed I moved the capital to Edirne, made peace with Byzantium and Venice, and focused on recovery, earning the title "Second Founder of the Ottoman Empire."

v.t: Zheng He's fourth voyage reaches East Africa
v.s: In 1416, Zheng He's fleet made its fourth voyage, reaching as far as Malindi (Kenya), Mogadishu (Somalia), and other East African ports. Zheng He brought back African exotic animals such as giraffes (qilin), zebras, and rhinos; African envoys also accompanied the fleet to China, promoting Sino-African exchanges.

d.e: the 15th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Zheng He's fifth voyage to the Western Ocean
v.s: In 1417, Zheng He made his fifth voyage, mainly to escort envoys from 20 countries back home. The fleet reached Hormuz in the Persian Gulf, Aden (Yemen), and Dhofar in southern Arabia. Through trade, Zheng He acquired large quantities of spices, jewels, and exotic animals, strengthening ties with West Asia.

v.t: Martin V elected pope
v.s: On November 11, 1417, the Council of Constance elected Oddone Colonna as Pope Martin V. The three rival popes (John XXIII, Benedict XIII, and Gregory XII) were deposed or resigned, ending the nearly 40-year Western Schism. Martin V returned to Rome in 1418, reestablishing the papal seat there.

d.e: the 16th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Portuguese discover the Madeira Islands
v.s: In 1418, Portuguese navigators João Gonçalves Zarco and Tristão Vaz Teixeira discovered the Madeira Islands. The following year, Portugal began colonizing the islands, planting sugarcane and grapes, and using them as a supply base for Atlantic exploration. This was Portugal's first overseas colony in the Atlantic.

v.t: Outbreak of the Jiaozhi independence war
v.s: In 1418, the Vietnamese noble Lê Lợi raised a rebellion against Ming rule at Lam Sơn (Thanh Hóa province), calling himself the Pacifying King. He gained widespread support, and although the Ming had numerical superiority, they were bogged down in guerrilla warfare. Lê Lợi persisted despite repeated defeats and eventually outlasted the Ming. The war lasted ten years and drained Ming resources.

d.e: the 17th year of the Yongle era

v.t: First Defenestration of Prague and outbreak of the Hussite Wars
v.s: On July 30, 1419, Hussite believers in Prague, led by priest Jan Želivský, stormed the New Town Hall and threw the burgomaster and councilors out the window – the First Defenestration of Prague. This sparked a revolt in Prague, and Hussite uprisings spread across Bohemia, beginning the Hussite Wars. The Hussites split into moderates (Utraquists) and radicals (Taborites). Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund organized crusades to suppress them, but the Hussite general Jan Žižka repeatedly defeated the imperial forces.

v.t: Prince Henry establishes a navigation school
v.s: In 1419, Prince Henry the Navigator of Portugal established a navigation school at Sagres, gathering astronomers, cartographers, and shipbuilders to improve navigation techniques and ship designs. He sponsored expeditions down the west African coast, laying the foundations for Portugal's Age of Discovery.

d.e: the 18th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Treaty of Troyes – English king becomes heir to French throne
v.s: On May 21, 1420, King Henry V of England and Queen Isabeau of France (acting for her mentally ill husband Charles VI) signed the Treaty of Troyes. The treaty declared Henry V as the heir to Charles VI, disinheriting the Dauphin Charles. Henry V then married Catherine of France. France was forced to cede large territories, and the Dauphin Charles retreated to Bourges to lead resistance. The treaty brought the Hundred Years' War to its high point in England's favor.

v.t: The Forbidden City in Beijing is essentially completed
v.s: In 1420, the Forbidden City in Beijing was largely completed, with over 980 buildings covering 720,000 square meters, including more than 70 major halls and some 9,000 rooms. At the end of the year, the Yongle Emperor announced the formal move of the capital to Beijing, and on New Year's Day 1421, he held a grand ceremony in the new Hall of Supreme Harmony. The Forbidden City was not only the pinnacle of ancient Chinese palace architecture but also the most magnificent palace complex in the world at the time.

v.t: Battle of Vítkov Hill during the Hussite Wars
v.s: On July 14, 1420, Jan Žižka commanded Hussite forces to repel the first crusade of Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund at Vítkov Hill east of Prague. Žižka used the wagon fort tactic: chaining carts together to form a defensive barrier armed with cannons and crossbows. Repeated crusader cavalry charges were beaten back with heavy losses. The battle consolidated Hussite control over Prague.

d.e: the 19th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Ming officially moves capital to Beijing
v.s: On February 2, 1421 (the first day of the lunar new year), the Yongle Emperor held a grand court assembly in the Hall of Supreme Harmony (now Taihe Hall), proclaiming Beijing as the official capital of the Ming dynasty. Nanjing was retained as a secondary capital with its own six ministries. The move strengthened Ming control over the northern frontier and established Beijing as China's political center for the next 500 years, but it also drained the treasury and burdened the populace.

v.t: Zheng He's sixth voyage to the Western Ocean
v.s: In 1421, Zheng He was ordered to make a sixth voyage, escorting envoys from 16 countries including Champa and Siam back home. The fleet visited Ceylon, Calicut, Hormuz, Dhofar, Aden, and Somalia. At Hormuz, Zheng He met Arab merchants and learned more about the Western world.

v.t: Fire destroys the three main halls of the Forbidden City
v.s: On May 9, 1421, the Hall of Supreme Harmony, the Hall of Central Harmony, and the Hall of Preserving Harmony were struck by lightning and burned to the ground. The Yongle Emperor believed it was a heavenly sign of punishment for moving the capital and became fearful. He then ordered the dismissal of officials who had opposed the move. The three halls were not rebuilt until 1440 (the 5th year of the Zhengtong era).

d.e: the 20th year of the Yongle era

v.t: Yongle Emperor's fifth personal campaign into the northern steppe
v.s: In 1422, the Yongle Emperor led his fifth personal campaign. The Tatar leader Arukh stopped paying tribute to the Ming, and Zhu Di personally led the army out of the passes. Ming forces defeated Arukh at Kaiping (present-day Zhenglan Banner, Inner Mongolia), and Arukh fled to the Oirats. On the return march, the emperor learned of a Jurchen rebellion in Liaodong and defeated them as well. This was Zhu Di's last campaign.

v.t: Battle of Brodnikowo
v.s: On September 27, 1422, Hussite forces under the blind general Jan Žižka defeated a German crusader army at Brodnikowo (present-day Brod, Czech Republic). Žižka's command was masterful, and Hussite field tactics reached their zenith.

d.e: the 21st year of the Yongle era

v.t: Construction begins on the dome of Florence Cathedral
v.s: In 1423, architect Filippo Brunelleschi began building the dome of the Basilica of Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence. He used a double-shell structure, herringbone brick pattern, and innovative hoisting machines to overcome the immense technical challenges. The dome had a span of 42 meters, the largest in the world at the time. Its completion marked the beginning of Renaissance architecture.

v.t: Malacca Sultanate formally established and converts to Islam
v.s: The port city of Malacca on the Malay Peninsula was formally founded as a sultanate under Parameswara, who converted to Islam. It quickly became a center of Southeast Asian trade, situated at the crossroads of the Indian Ocean and South China Sea, attracting merchants from India, Arabia, Persia, and China. Zheng He's fleet visited Malacca many times, and the Yongle Emperor appointed Parameswara as King of Malacca.

d.e: the 22nd year of the Yongle era

v.t: Yongle Emperor Zhu Di dies
v.s: On August 12, 1424, the Yongle Emperor died at Yumuchuan (northwest of present-day Duolun, Inner Mongolia) while returning from his fifth northern campaign. He was 65. During his 22-year reign, he moved the capital, compiled the Yongle Encyclopedia, sent Zheng He on his voyages, and led five personal campaigns, establishing the Yongle prosperity. After his death, the eunuch Ma Yun and Grand Scholar Yang Rong kept the news secret and hurried back to Beijing to report.

v.t: Hongxi Emperor ascends the throne
v.s: On September 7, 1424, Zhu Di's eldest son Zhu Gaozhi ascended the throne in Beijing as the Hongxi Emperor. He reversed his father's aggressive policies, stopping Zheng He's voyages and the Annam campaign, reducing court expenses, pardoning surviving Jianwen loyalists, and adopting a policy of rest with the people. This ushered in the "Ren-Xuan Golden Age."

d.e: the 1st year of the Hongxi era

v.t: Hongxi Emperor suddenly dies; Xuande Emperor ascends
v.s: On May 29, 1425, the Hongxi Emperor died suddenly after only 10 months on the throne, at age 48. The cause is uncertain – possibly a heart attack or from overindulgence. His eldest son Zhu Zhanji was recalled from Nanjing and ascended as the Xuande Emperor. Zhu Gaoxu, the Prince of Han, had attempted to ambush and kill Zhu Zhanji en route but failed; he later rebelled in 1426 and was quickly pacified by the Xuande Emperor.

v.t: University of Leuven founded
v.s: In 1425, Pope Martin V approved the establishment of a university in Leuven, in the Duchy of Brabant. The University of Leuven became the oldest and most important university in the Low Countries, a center for Catholic theology, philosophy, and medicine. The famous scholar Erasmus studied there.

d.e: the 1st year of the Xuande era

v.t: Rebellion of Zhu Gaoxu, Prince of Han
v.s: In August 1426, the Xuande Emperor's uncle, Zhu Gaoxu, Prince of Han, raised a rebellion at Le'an Prefecture (present-day Huimin, Shandong). Zhu Gaoxu had coveted the heir-apparent position during his father's reign and attempted to emulate his father's seizure of power. The Xuande Emperor personally led a large army to besiege Le'an. The rebels' morale collapsed, and Zhu Gaoxu surrendered and was captured; he later died in captivity.

v.t: Portuguese captain passes Cape Bojador
v.s: In 1426, the captain Gil Eanes, sent by Prince Henry the Navigator, sailed down the west coast of Africa and finally passed Cape Bojador (previously considered impassable by Europeans). His ship returned safely with a small cargo of African goods, breaking the fear of the southern sea. Over the following decades, Portuguese explorers continued pushing south along the African coast.

d.e: the 2nd year of the Xuande era

v.t: Ming withdraws from Jiaozhi; Lê Lợi establishes Later Lê dynasty
v.s: In 1427, the Xuande Emperor decided to abandon Jiaozhi (Annam). Lê Lợi's rebel army had grown, and the Ming were bogged down. The Ming commander Wang Tong negotiated a withdrawal agreement with Lê Lợi. In 1428, Lê Lợi established the Later Lê dynasty with its capital at Hanoi, proclaiming himself Grand Progenitor. He undertook reforms, restored the civil service examinations, and codified laws, bringing Vietnam relative stability. The Ming recognized Lê Lợi's rule.

v.t: Duke Philip the Good of Burgundy rules the Low Countries
v.s: By 1427, Duke Philip the Good of Burgundy had, through inheritance and conquest, gained control over most of the Low Countries (present-day Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg), making Brussels his administrative center. He strengthened centralization and promoted commerce and industry, making the Burgundian court one of the richest and most cultured in Europe.

d.e: the 3rd year of the Xuande era

v.t: Aztec Triple Alliance formed
v.s: In 1428, three city-states in the Valley of Mexico – Tenochtitlan (Aztec), Texcoco, and Tlacopan – formed an alliance to overthrow the ruling Tepanec Empire. The three then rapidly expanded, with the Aztecs becoming dominant. The Aztec Empire (Mexica Empire) established a tribute system and became the most powerful state in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica.

v.t: Joan of Arc first meets the Dauphin of France
v.s: In 1428, the 17-year-old French girl Joan of Arc approached the garrison commander at Vaucouleurs and asked to be taken to the Dauphin Charles at Chinon. She claimed to hear the voices of saints who foretold that France would defeat the English. After several refusals, she was eventually given the chance.

d.e: the 4th year of the Xuande era

v.t: Joan of Arc lifts the Siege of Orléans
v.s: From April to May 1429, the 17-year-old Joan of Arc led French forces to lift the English siege of Orléans after seven months. Joan charged into battle holding her banner, inspiring the French troops. On May 8, the English withdrew. The Battle of Orléans was a turning point in the Hundred Years' War, breaking the myth of English invincibility. Joan became known as the "Maid of Orléans."

v.t: Charles VII crowned at Reims Cathedral
v.s: On July 17, 1429, Joan accompanied the Dauphin Charles through English-held territory to Reims, where he was crowned King Charles VII of France. The coronation greatly enhanced Charles's legitimacy and French morale. Joan stood beside the king holding her banner. Thereafter, Charles VII began reconquering his kingdom.

v.t: Battle of Patay
v.s: On June 18, 1429, the French army won the Battle of Patay against an English relief force. French cavalry charged the English longbowmen, killing about 2,500 English. This was the first time in the Hundred Years' War that the French had decisively beaten the English in open battle, demonstrating the restoration of the French heavy cavalry.

d.e: the 5th year of the Xuande era

v.t: Joan of Arc captured by the Burgundians
v.s: On May 23, 1430, while fighting Burgundian forces outside Compiègne, Joan of Arc was captured when the town gate was closed prematurely. Duke Philip the Good of Burgundy sold her to the English for 10,000 francs. The English then handed her over to an ecclesiastical court in Rouen.

v.t: Zheng He's seventh voyage to the Western Ocean
v.s: In 1430, the Xuande Emperor, seeking to revive the tributary system, ordered Zheng He to undertake a seventh and final voyage. The fleet reached Hormuz, Dhofar, Aden, Mogadishu, and over 30 other countries. However, Zheng He died at Calicut on the return voyage in 1433. After the fleet returned, the Ming officially stopped the voyages.

v.t: Medici family begins to dominate Florence
v.s: By 1430, the banker Cosimo de' Medici, through his immense wealth and political maneuvering, gradually came to control the Florentine Republic. He sponsored the arts and built public works, making Florence a center of the Renaissance. The Medici family would rule Florence for three centuries.

d.e: the 6th year of the Xuande era

v.t: Joan of Arc burned at the stake
v.s: On May 30, 1431, at the age of 19, Joan of Arc was burned at the stake in the Old Market Square of Rouen, convicted of heresy and witchcraft. The English-dominated ecclesiastical court was presided over by Bishop Pierre Cauchon. On the pyre, Joan called out the name of Jesus and asked for a cross, moving the crowd. Her death inflamed French nationalism and made her a symbol of French resistance. The Catholic Church canonized her as a saint in 1920.

v.t: Angkor abandoned
v.s: In 1431, the Siamese (Thai) kingdom of Ayutthaya captured and sacked the Khmer capital of Angkor. The Khmer king Ponhea Yat abandoned Angkor and moved the capital to Phnom Penh. The great city was swallowed by the jungle and not rediscovered until the 19th century. The Khmer Empire declined, and Siam became the dominant power in mainland Southeast Asia.

v.t: Council of Basel opens
v.s: In 1431, after the death of Pope Martin V, the newly elected Pope Eugene IV convened the Council of Basel to pursue church reform. The council soon divided, with some delegates advocating curbs on papal power, leading to a protracted conflict with the pope.

d.e: the 7th year of the Xuande era

v.t: Portuguese discover the Azores
v.s: In 1432, the Portuguese navigator Gonçalo Velho Cabral discovered most of the islands of the Azores. They were later colonized and became an important stopping point for Portuguese Atlantic exploration and a springboard for crossing the Atlantic to the Americas.

v.t: Pachacuti rises in the Inca Empire
v.s: In 1432, the Inca Empire was still in its early expansion phase. The future great reformer Pachacuti (who would ascend around 1438) had not yet taken power, but the Incas were already expanding outward. No major events are recorded for this year, but Inca civilization continued developing its road and agricultural systems.

d.e: the 8th year of the Xuande era

v.t: Zheng He dies on the return of his seventh voyage
v.s: In 1433, Zheng He died at Calicut (present-day Kozhikode, India) on the return journey of his seventh voyage, at about age 62. After the fleet returned to Nanjing, the voyages were officially halted. Zheng He's seven voyages spanned 28 years, visited more than 30 countries and regions, and were a monumental feat in world maritime history.

v.t: Rise of the Songhai Empire
v.s: In 1433, the Songhai Empire began to rise under the chieftain Sonni Ali. Songhai was formerly a vassal of the Mali Empire, but now took advantage of Mali's decline to assert independence and expand. Sonni Ali (who would take the throne in 1464) would later build a powerful Songhai Empire that replaced Mali as the dominant power in West Africa.

d.e: the 9th year of the Xuande era

v.t: Medici family assumes power in Florence
v.s: In 1434, Cosimo de' Medici returned from exile and, with popular support and banking wealth, effectively controlled the Florentine government. The Medici began their 300-year rule over Florence (with brief interruptions). Cosimo and his grandson Lorenzo were great patrons of the arts, supporting Donatello, Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Leonardo da Vinci, earning the title "Godfathers of the Renaissance."

v.t: Hussite Wars end
v.s: On May 30, 1434, the moderate Hussites (Utraquists) allied with Catholic forces defeated the radical Taborites at the Battle of Lipany. The radical leader Prokop the Great was killed, and the Hussite Wars largely ended. The Utraquists reached a compromise with the Church, allowing the Hussite church to survive in Bohemia while returning to the Catholic fold.

d.e: the 1st year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Xuande Emperor dies; young Zhu Qizhen ascends throne
v.s: On January 31, 1435, the Xuande Emperor died at age 38. His son Zhu Qizhen, only 9, ascended as the Zhengtong Emperor. The Grand Empress Dowager Zhang (wife of the Hongxi Emperor) acted as regent, relying on the "Three Yangs" (Yang Shiqi, Yang Rong, Yang Pu) to continue the golden age of the Ren-Xuan era. However, the eunuch Wang Zhen began to gain favor, setting the stage for the later Tumu Crisis.

v.t: Aragon unites with Naples
v.s: In 1435, King Alfonso V of Aragon won the war for the Neapolitan throne and formally occupied Naples in 1442, making it his court. The Crown of Aragon became a Mediterranean power.

d.e: the 1st year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Ming begins building brick and stone sections of the Great Wall
v.s: In 1436, the Ming began large-scale reinforcement of the northern frontier by building brick and stone sections of the Great Wall, replacing older rammed-earth walls. The Ming Great Wall stretched from the Yalu River in the east to Jiayuguan in the west, over 8,800 km. After the Yongle era, the Ming adopted a defensive strategy, and the Great Wall became the primary barrier against Mongol incursions.

v.t: Pachacuti ascends to the Inca throne
v.s: In 1436 (actual year often given as 1438), the Inca prince Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui ascended the throne after defeating the invading Chanca people and formally established the Inca Empire. He undertook comprehensive reforms: rebuilding Cusco, establishing a centralized administrative system, promoting Quechua, and organizing extensive terracing and road construction. He is called the "Alexander the Great of the Incas."

d.e: the 2nd year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Albert II of Habsburg elected Holy Roman Emperor
v.s: In 1437, the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund died, and his son-in-law Albert II (of the Habsburg family) was elected King of the Romans, crowned in 1438. The Habsburgs thereafter virtually monopolized the imperial crown until the empire's dissolution.

v.t: Trade conflicts between Ming and the Oirats
v.s: In 1437, the Oirat leader Toghon united the Mongol tribes and clashed with the Ming over horse market trade. The Ming restricted iron exports, while the Oirats raided the border. Relations soured, foreshadowing the later Tumu Crisis.

d.e: the 3rd year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Pachacuti's reforms and the establishment of the Inca Empire
v.s: In 1438 (commonly accepted date), Inca ruler Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui, after defeating the Chanca, formally established the Inca Empire. He rebuilt Cusco, set up a four-province centralized system, built a 40,000 km network of roads and waystations, and organized extensive terracing and irrigation works. He is known as the "Alexander of the Incas."

v.t: Expansion of the Aztec Empire
v.s: In 1438, under Moctezuma I (who ascended around 1440), the Aztec Empire began large-scale expansion, conquering the Valley of Mexico and beyond, establishing a tribute system. The Aztecs became the most powerful military empire in Mesoamerica.

d.e: the 4th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Council of Florence
v.s: In 1439, the Council of Florence met to attempt healing the schism between the Catholic and Orthodox churches. It issued the decree of union (Laetentur Caeli), and the Orthodox representatives accepted papal primacy, but upon returning to Constantinople they faced strong opposition, and the union ultimately failed, reflecting the irreconcilable differences between the two churches.

v.t: King Sejong of Korea promotes Hangul
v.s: In 1439, King Sejong the Great continued to promote the Hangul alphabet, which he had created in 1443. This writing system, scientifically designed based on speech organs, enabled common people to read and write, greatly promoting cultural literacy in Korea. However, it faced resistance from the aristocracy and did not become widely used until the 16th century.

d.e: the 5th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Gutenberg invents European movable-type printing
v.s: Around 1440, German goldsmith Johannes Gutenberg invented metal movable-type printing in Mainz. He used lead-tin-antimony alloy to create reusable type, improved the printing press, and developed oil-based ink. The printing of the 42-line Bible (Gutenberg Bible) in 1455 marked the beginning of the printing revolution. This invention enabled mass book production, greatly accelerating the spread of knowledge, the Reformation, and the Scientific Revolution.

v.t: Guru Nanak, founder of Sikhism, born
v.s: [Correction: Guru Nanak was actually born in 1469. No major event in 1440 related to Sikhism. The entry is kept as a placeholder but corrected.]

v.t: Reconstruction of the Three Great Halls of the Forbidden City completed
v.s: In 1440, the reconstruction of the Three Great Halls – the Hall of Supreme Harmony, Hall of Central Harmony, and Hall of Preserving Harmony – in the Forbidden City was completed after 19 years. The new halls were built on a larger scale than the originals, establishing the basic layout of today's halls.

d.e: the 6th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Portugal begins African slave trade
v.s: In 1441, Portuguese explorer Antão Gonçalves brought back ten black slaves from the West African coast, starting the direct European involvement in the Atlantic slave trade. Thereafter, the Portuguese continuously purchased or captured slaves from West Africa to be sold in Europe and later in the Americas, causing deep and lasting damage to African societies.

v.t: Ming salt law reforms
v.s: In 1441, due to fiscal difficulties, the Ming implemented salt law reforms allowing merchants to exchange grain for salt certificates (the Kaizhong method). This system encouraged merchants to transport grain to border garrisons in exchange for salt trading rights, benefiting frontier defense and commerce, though later abuses appeared.

d.e: the 7th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Portuguese reach Mauritania
v.s: In 1442, Portuguese navigator Nuno Tristão sailed along the West African coast as far as Arguin Bay in Mauritania, establishing the first European trading post in Africa for exchanging gold, ivory, and slaves. This marked the beginning of European penetration into the African interior.

v.t: Grand Empress Dowager Zhang of Ming dies
v.s: In 1442, the Grand Empress Dowager Zhang, grandmother of the Zhengtong Emperor, died. She had been a guardian of the Ren-Xuan golden age. After her death, the eunuch Wang Zhen was no longer constrained and began to meddle in government, leading to increasing corruption in Ming politics.

d.e: the 8th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Skanderbeg's Albanian rebellion
v.s: In November 1443, the Albanian national hero George Castrioti (Skanderbeg) raised the banner of revolt against the Ottomans at Krujë. He had been an Ottoman officer but now seized Krujë Castle with 300 Albanians and established an independent Albanian principality. For over two decades, he repeatedly repelled Ottoman armies, earning the title "Dragon of Albania."

v.t: King Sejong of Korea promulgates the Hunmin Jeongeum
v.s: In 1443 (or 1446), King Sejong the Great promulgated the Hunmin Jeongeum (the Correct Sounds for the Instruction of the People), creating the Korean alphabet. This phonetic script originally consisted of 28 letters (later reduced to 24), making literacy accessible to commoners. It faced opposition from the aristocracy and did not come into widespread use until the 16th century. Today, Hangul is recognized as one of the most scientific writing systems.

d.e: the 9th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Battle of Varna – Ottoman victory over crusaders
v.s: On November 10, 1444, Ottoman Sultan Murad II crushed a European crusade led by King Władysław III of Poland at Varna (present-day Bulgaria). The Polish king was killed, and the crusader army disintegrated. The battle solidified Ottoman control over the Balkans, and Europe was powerless to stop their expansion.

v.t: Ming northern campaign against the Oirats
v.s: In 1444, the Zhengtong Emperor sent an army north against the Oirats, but with limited success. The eunuch Wang Zhen began to take control of military affairs, laying the groundwork for the Tumu Crisis of 1449.

d.e: the 10th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Esen Tayisi succeeds as Oirat leader
v.s: In 1445, the Oirat leader Toghon died and was succeeded by his son Esen. Ambitious and having united the Mongol tribes, Esen constantly raided the Ming border and demanded increased tribute. He later launched the invasion that led to the Tumu Crisis.

v.t: Portuguese merchants reach Senegal
v.s: In 1445, Portuguese navigator Dinis Dias (brother of Bartolomeu Dias) reached the mouth of the Senegal River, where he traded with local black kingdoms. They found abundant gold, ivory, and pepper, further spurring the drive to explore southward.

d.e: the 11th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Korea officially promulgates the Hunmin Jeongeum
v.s: In 1446, King Sejong formally promulgated the Hunmin Jeongeum and wrote the "Hunmin Jeongeum Haerye" (commentary). Despite opposition from Confucian scholars, Hangul became the cultural foundation of the Korean people and is still used in both South and North Korea.

v.t: Peace treaty between Venice and the Ottomans
v.s: In 1446, the Republic of Venice and the Ottoman Empire signed a peace treaty recognizing Ottoman territorial gains in the Balkans while allowing Venice to retain its commercial outposts in the Aegean and Adriatic. Venice prioritized trade interests and maintained long-term peace with the Ottomans.

d.e: the 12th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Eunuch Wang Zhen monopolizes Ming government
v.s: In 1447, the eunuch Wang Zhen fully seized control of the Ming court. He sidelined loyal officials, engaged in bribery and corruption, and compromised with Esen's provocations. Wang Zhen's dominance marked a major turning point in the Ming dynasty's decline.

v.t: Frederick III crowned Holy Roman Emperor
v.s: In 1447, Frederick III of the House of Habsburg was crowned Holy Roman Emperor (formally by the pope in 1452). He reigned for 53 years, one of the longest-reigning emperors in the empire's history.

d.e: the 13th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Second Battle of Kosovo
v.s: In October 1448, Ottoman Sultan Murad II defeated a crusader army led by Hungarian regent John Hunyadi on the Kosovo Field. The battle cemented Ottoman control over the Balkans, and Hungary could no longer mount major counteroffensives.

v.t: France recaptures Rouen
v.s: In 1448, King Charles VII of France recovered Rouen, further reducing English holdings in Normandy. The French used gunpowder artillery and a standing army to gradually push the English out of France.

d.e: the 14th year of the Zhengtong era

v.t: Tumu Crisis – Zhengtong Emperor captured
v.s: In July 1449, the Oirat leader Esen launched a major invasion. The eunuch Wang Zhen persuaded the 23-year-old Zhengtong Emperor to lead a personal campaign. The hastily assembled Ming army of 500,000 (claimed) reached Datong, then received word of a vanguard defeat. Wang Zhen ordered a retreat. On the return, Wang Zhen wanted the emperor to pass by his hometown (Wei County, modern Yu County, Hebei) to show off, causing the army to detour. On August 31, the Ming army was encircled by Oirat cavalry at Tumu Fort (southeast of present-day Huailai, Hebei). Esen feigned negotiation, and as the thirsty Ming forces moved to fetch water, they were ambushed and annihilated. The Zhengtong Emperor was captured; Wang Zhen was killed by the enraged officer Fan Zhong; over 50 senior officials and generals died. It was the most disastrous defeat in Ming history.

v.t: Defense of Beijing – Yu Qian saves the Ming
v.s: After the Tumu Crisis, Esen advanced on Beijing with the captured Zhengtong Emperor. The Minister of War, Yu Qian, strongly opposed moving the capital south and instead supported the enthronement of the emperor's brother Zhu Qiyu as the Jingtai Emperor. He organized 220,000 troops and civilians to defend Beijing. In October 1449, Yu Qian commanded the defense in person, using cannons to repulse the Oirats at Desheng Gate. Realizing the Ming had a new emperor and that his captive was useless, Esen withdrew north. The Defense of Beijing saved the Ming dynasty, but the capture of the emperor was a lasting humiliation.

d.e: the 1st year of the Jingtai era

v.t: Oirats release the Zhengtong Emperor
v.s: In 1450, Esen, unable to make headway against the Ming and facing increased conflict with other Mongol tribes, decided to release the captured Zhengtong Emperor to ease relations. In August, the emperor returned to Beijing but was placed under house arrest in the Southern Palace by the Jingtai Emperor, who gave him the title of Retired Emperor. This set the stage for the later "Seizure of the Gate."

v.t: France recaptures Normandy
v.s: In 1450, French forces recaptured Caen, the capital of Normandy. By the end of the Hundred Years' War, France had regained almost all of its territory except Calais. English rule in France collapsed.

v.t: Gutenberg begins printing the Bible
v.s: Around 1450, Gutenberg began printing the 42-line Bible in Mainz, completing it in 1455. It was the first major printed book in the West, with about 180 copies produced. Surviving copies are among the most valuable books in the world.

v.t: Mutapa Empire controls southeastern Africa
v.s: Around 1450, the Mutapa Empire (in present-day Zimbabwe and Mozambique) succeeded Great Zimbabwe as the dominant power in southeastern Africa. The Mutapa king (called Mwene Mutapa) controlled the gold trade between the interior and the Indian Ocean. The Portuguese arrived around 1512 and tried to control the trade but met fierce resistance. Mutapa's decline came from internal civil wars and Portuguese infiltration in the 17th century.

d.e: the 2nd year of the Jingtai era

v.t: Mehmed II becomes Ottoman sultan
v.s: In 1451, Sultan Murad II died and was succeeded by his son Mehmed II. The 19-year-old Mehmed resolved to capture Constantinople. He greatly expanded the army, built giant cannons, and launched the siege in 1453. His accession marked a new phase of Ottoman expansion.

v.t: Ming compiles the Huan Yu Tong Zhi
v.s: In 1451, the Jingtai Emperor ordered the compilation of the Huan Yu Tong Zhi, a national geographical gazetteer. Completed in 1455, it was later reworked by Li Xian in 1461 as the Da Ming Yi Tong Zhi.

d.e: the 3rd year of the Jingtai era

v.t: Leonardo da Vinci born
v.s: On April 15, 1452, Leonardo da Vinci was born in Vinci, Italy. He was a true Renaissance polymath, excelling in painting, sculpture, architecture, science, and invention. His masterpieces, the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper, are treasures of world art.

v.t: France recaptures Bordeaux
v.s: In 1452 (actual events in 1453), the Hundred Years' War drew to a close. After the French victory at the Battle of Castillon (July 1453), France recaptured Bordeaux, the last English stronghold in Gascony, in October 1453. England retained only Calais on the continent. The Hundred Years' War ended.

d.e: the 4th year of the Jingtai era

v.t: Fall of Constantinople – end of the Eastern Roman Empire
v.s: On April 6, 1453, Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II laid siege to Constantinople with about 80,000 troops. Byzantine Emperor Constantine XI held out with 7,000 defenders (including Genoese and Venetian volunteers). Mehmed used the huge "Basilica" cannon to batter the walls and even dragged his ships overland into the Golden Horn. On May 29, the Ottomans launched a final assault; the Genoese commander Giovanni Giustiniani was wounded and withdrew, leading to the breach of the walls. Constantine XI died fighting. Constantinople was renamed Istanbul and became the Ottoman capital. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire fell after 1,123 years. Traditionally, this event marks the end of the Middle Ages and the beginning of the early modern period.

v.t: End of the Hundred Years' War (Battle of Castillon)
v.s: On July 17, 1453, the French used massed artillery (the first decisive artillery battle) to annihilate the English at the Battle of Castillon. England lost all its French territories except Calais. The Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) ended in French victory. French national consciousness awakened, and the process of centralization began.

d.e: the 5th year of the Jingtai era

v.t: War between the Teutonic Order and Poland resumes
v.s: In February 1454, the Prussian Confederation (an alliance of Prussian cities against the Teutonic Order) allied with Poland and declared war on the Order, starting the Thirteen Years' War. The war ended in Polish victory, with the Order ceding western Prussia to Poland.

v.t: Ming establishes the Taicang Silver Reserve
v.s: In 1454, the Ming established the Taicang Silver Reserve as a national fiscal repository. Silver gradually became the main currency in the Ming, laying the foundation for the Single Whip Law reforms later under Zhang Juzheng.

d.e: the 6th year of the Jingtai era

v.t: First Battle of St Albans – Wars of the Roses begin
v.s: On May 22, 1455, Richard, Duke of York, led his forces to victory over the Lancastrians at St Albans, marking the beginning of the Wars of the Roses. The war pitted the House of York (white rose) against the House of Lancaster (red rose) for the English throne. The conflict lasted 30 years, killing many nobles and ending with the victory of Henry Tudor (Lancaster), who established the Tudor dynasty.

v.t: Gutenberg Bible completed
v.s: In 1455, Gutenberg completed the printing of the 42-line Bible in Mainz, with about 180 copies produced. This was the first major book printed using movable type in Europe, marking the beginning of the printing age.

d.e: the 7th year of the Jingtai era

v.t: Siege of Belgrade – Hunyadi defeats the Ottomans
v.s: In July 1456, Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II laid siege to Belgrade. The Hungarian regent John Hunyadi and the Franciscan friar John of Capistrano led the defenders and crusaders in a stubborn resistance. On July 22, the defenders sortied and routed the Ottomans; Mehmed was wounded and withdrew. The victory halted Ottoman expansion into Central Europe for 70 years. Hunyadi died of plague shortly afterward.

v.t: Ming resumes tribute trade with the Oirats
v.s: In 1456, the Ming restored tribute market relations with Esen's Oirats. The Oirats offered horses to the Ming, who gave presents in return and opened border markets, bringing temporary stability to the northern frontier.

d.e: the 1st year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Seizure of the Gate – Restoration of the Tianshun Emperor
v.s: In the first month of 1457, the Jingtai Emperor fell seriously ill. Generals Shi Heng, Xu Youzhen, and eunuch Cao Jixiang staged a coup, bringing the Retired Emperor Zhu Qizhen from the Southern Palace to the throne. Zhu Qizhen re-ascended, changing the era name to Tianshun. The Jingtai Emperor was deposed and soon died (some say he was poisoned). Yu Qian, who had supported Jingtai, was executed on fabricated treason charges. After the Seizure of the Gate, Ming politics became even more corrupt.

v.t: Battle of Stirling Bridge (Wars of the Roses)
v.s: In 1457, the Wars of the Roses continued with fighting along the Welsh border and northern England. No major battles took place this year, but the Yorkist cause was gaining strength.

d.e: the 2nd year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Matthias Corvinus elected King of Hungary
v.s: In 1458, the Hungarian nobility elected John Hunyadi's son Matthias Corvinus as king, at age 15. He later became one of the most brilliant rulers of the Renaissance, creating a standing mercenary army (the Black Army) and conquering parts of Austria.

v.t: Portuguese exploration advances
v.s: In 1458, Prince Henry the Navigator sent expeditions down the West African coast, reaching the Guinea coast. They discovered abundant pepper, ivory, and gold, and Portugal began direct trade with local black kingdoms.

d.e: the 3rd year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Battle of Blore Heath (Wars of the Roses)
v.s: On September 23, 1459, the Yorkists defeated the Lancastrians at Blore Heath. However, on October 12, the Lancastrians surrounded the Yorkists near Ludlow, and Richard, Duke of York, fled to Ireland. The Wars of the Roses intensified.

v.t: Ottomans conquer Serbia
v.s: In 1459, the Ottoman Empire captured the Serbian capital Smederevo, extinguishing Serbia as an independent kingdom and turning it into an Ottoman province. Serbia was one of the last major Balkan states to resist the Ottomans.

d.e: the 4th year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Battle of Northampton (Wars of the Roses)
v.s: On July 10, 1460, the Yorkists defeated the Lancastrians at Northampton and captured King Henry VI. Richard, Duke of York, returned to England and claimed the throne, but the nobles only agreed to make him Henry's heir (and protector). This enraged Queen Margaret (Lancastrian).

v.t: Portuguese discover the Cape Verde Islands
v.s: In 1460, Portuguese navigator Diogo Gomes discovered the Cape Verde Islands. These islands later became important Portuguese waystations and slave trading posts.

v.t: Ming cracks down on the White Lotus Society
v.s: In 1460, the Tianshun Emperor ordered a severe investigation of popular religious groups like the White Lotus Society. The White Lotus Society, which had repeatedly revolted against oppression, was seen as a major threat by the Ming.

d.e: the 5th year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Battle of Towton and establishment of the Yorkist dynasty
v.s: On March 29, 1461, the Yorkists under Edward (son of Richard of York) defeated the Lancastrians at Towton in a snowstorm. About 50,000 men were engaged – one of the largest battles ever fought on English soil. The Yorkists won; Lancastrian losses were heavy. Edward entered London and proclaimed himself King Edward IV, founding the Yorkist dynasty. Henry VI and Queen Margaret fled to Scotland.

v.t: Conspiracy of Cao Jixiang in the Ming
v.s: In August 1461, the eunuch Cao Jixiang and his adoptive son Cao Qin launched a coup in the capital, attempting to seize the throne. The Tianshun Emperor quickly suppressed the revolt, and the Cao family was executed. The incident highlighted the dangers of eunuch domination.

d.e: the 6th year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Ivan III becomes Grand Prince of Moscow
v.s: In 1462, Ivan III (Ivan the Great) succeeded as Grand Prince of Moscow. He was the greatest ruler of the Moscow principality, during whose reign (1462-1505) he conquered Novgorod, overthrew the Golden Horde, married a Byzantine princess, assumed the title of Tsar, and laid the foundations of a centralized Russian state.

v.t: Ottomans conquer the Peloponnese
v.s: In 1462, the Ottoman Empire completed the conquest of the Morea (Peloponnese peninsula), extinguishing the last Byzantine remnant. Venice's colonies in Greece faced enormous threats.

d.e: the 7th year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Ottomans conquer Bosnia
v.s: In 1463, the Ottoman Empire captured the Bosnian capital of Jajce, destroying the Bosnian kingdom and incorporating it into Ottoman territory. Many Bosnian nobles converted to Islam, forming Europe's only indigenous Muslim nation (Bosniaks).

v.t: Venetian-Ottoman War (1463)
v.s: In 1463, the Republic of Venice declared war on the Ottoman Empire in an attempt to protect its Greek and Aegean colonies. The war lasted 16 years, ending with Venice ceding some territories.

d.e: the 8th year of the Tianshun era

v.t: Tianshun Emperor dies and abolishes human sacrifice
v.s: On February 23, 1464, the Tianshun Emperor died at age 38. In his will, he ordered the abolition of the practice of forcing concubines to be buried alive (human sacrifice) that had been introduced by the Hongwu Emperor. Although he had caused the Tumu Crisis and executed Yu Qian after his restoration, this final act partly redeemed his reputation. His son Zhu Jianshen ascended as the Chenghua Emperor.

v.t: Sonni Ali begins his conquests for the Songhai Empire
v.s: In 1464, Sonni Ali became king of the Songhai Empire. He led his army to conquer the remnants of the Mali Empire, capturing Timbuktu and making Songhai the new hegemon of West Africa.

v.t: Rise of the Songhai Empire: West Africa's largest empire
v.s: In 1464, Sonni Ali became king of the Songhai Empire and began massive military expansion. He built a powerful river fleet (Niger River flotilla) and captured Timbuktu and Djenné. In 1493, Muhammad Ture (Askia Muhammad) seized the throne, perfected the administrative system, and promoted Islam. The Songhai Empire became the largest empire in West African history, stretching from the Atlantic to central Niger. It collapsed after being defeated by Moroccan musketeers in 1591.

d.e: the 1st year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Revolt of the displaced peasants in Jingxiang, Ming
v.s: In 1465, heavy land taxes and land concentration drove large numbers of displaced peasants to the Jingxiang mountain region (bordering Hubei, Shaanxi, and Henan). Liu Tong and Shi Long led a revolt, claiming a million followers, but it was suppressed the next year. The Ming then established Yunyang Prefecture to strengthen control.

v.t: Wattasid dynasty established in Morocco
v.s: In 1465, the Wattasids seized power from the faltering Marinid dynasty in Morocco, establishing the Wattasid dynasty. However, the dynasty was weak, and Portugal took advantage to capture several coastal cities in Morocco.

d.e: the 2nd year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Treaty of Thorn ends the Thirteen Years' War
v.s: In October 1466, Poland and the Teutonic Order signed the Second Treaty of Thorn, ending the Thirteen Years' War. The Order ceded western Prussia (including Danzig) to Poland, while eastern Prussia became a Polish fief. The Grand Master moved to Königsberg, and the Order declined.

v.t: Ming establishes the Western Depot
v.s: In 1466, the Chenghua Emperor established the Western Depot, headed by the eunuch Wang Zhi. The Western Depot had even greater powers than the Eastern Depot and the Embroidered Uniform Guard, arresting ministers at will and spreading terror. It was abolished in 1482, but set a dangerous precedent for Ming secret police.

d.e: the 3rd year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Ōnin War begins – start of the Sengoku period in Japan
v.s: In 1467, a succession dispute for the shogunate in the Ashikaga shogunate led to civil war between the factions of Yamana Sōzen (Mochitoyo) and Hosokawa Katsumoto, fighting in Kyoto – the Ōnin War. The conflict spread across the country, the shogunate's authority collapsed, and provincial daimyō became independent. Japan entered the Sengoku period (Warring States) which lasted about 150 years (1467-1615).

v.t: Rise of the Jianzhou Jurchens in Ming
v.s: In 1467 (the 3rd year of Chenghua), the Jianzhou Jurchen tribe emerged under the leadership of headman Dongshan. The Jianzhou Jurchens lived in the Hun River and Suzi River basins (present-day Xinbin, Liaoning). The Ming pursued a policy of divide and rule and inducement, but the momentum toward Jurchen unification was unstoppable.

d.e: the 4th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Charles the Bold becomes Duke of Burgundy
v.s: In 1468, Charles the Bold became Duke of Burgundy. He aimed to raise Burgundy to an independent kingdom, fiercely opposing King Louis XI of France. Charles possessed the Low Countries and the Burgundian homeland, making him the richest prince in Europe.

v.t: Ming suppresses the Yao uprising in Guangxi
v.s: In 1468, the Ming sent Han Yong with a large army to suppress a Yao uprising in the Great Vine Mountain region of Guangxi. The rebel leader Hou Dagou was captured and killed. After the suppression, the Ming tightened control over the southwestern chieftains.

d.e: the 5th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Unification of Spain – marriage of Isabella and Ferdinand
v.s: On October 19, 1469, Princess Isabella of Castile married Prince Ferdinand of Aragon. The marriage laid the foundation for the future unification of Spain. In 1474, Isabella became Queen of Castile; in 1479, Ferdinand inherited the throne of Aragon, and the two kingdoms merged into the Kingdom of Spain. They completed the Reconquista and sponsored Columbus's voyages.

v.t: Lorenzo de' Medici takes power in Florence
v.s: In 1469, Piero de' Medici died and was succeeded by his sons Lorenzo (Lorenzo the Magnificent) and Giuliano, who jointly ruled Florence. Lorenzo was the greatest patron of the Renaissance, supporting Botticelli, Michelangelo, Leonardo da Vinci, and others, making Florence the art center of Europe.

d.e: the 6th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Edward IV restored (Wars of the Roses)
v.s: In 1470, Edward IV fell out with the Earl of Warwick (the Kingmaker) and was briefly deposed, with Henry VI restored. But Edward IV raised a new army and counterattacked the next year, regaining the throne.

v.t: Ming builds the Great Wall
v.s: In 1470, the Ming began large-scale construction of the Great Wall from Juyong Pass to Shanhaiguan. The present-day sections of the Great Wall around Beijing were largely formed then. The Ming continually reinforced the Wall against Mongol threats.

v.t: Inca conquest of the Chimú Kingdom
v.s: In 1470, Topa Inca Yupanqui, son of Pachacuti, conquered the Chimú Kingdom on the northern coast of Peru. The Chimú had a population of about 200,000; their irrigation works and metallurgy were inherited by the Incas. The Inca Empire expanded to Ecuador and central Chile.

d.e: the 7th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Battles of Barnet and Tewkesbury – Yorkist dynasty secured
v.s: On April 14, 1471, Edward IV defeated and killed the Earl of Warwick at Barnet. On May 4, he defeated Queen Margaret's Lancastrian army at Tewkesbury; the Lancastrian Prince Edward was killed. Edward IV returned to London, and Henry VI was secretly murdered. The Yorkist dynasty was temporarily secure.

v.t: Ming bans private overseas trade
v.s: In 1471, the Chenghua Emperor reaffirmed the maritime ban, strictly forbidding private overseas trade and permitting only tributary trade. The ban led to rampant smuggling along the coast and worsened the wokou (pirate) problem.

d.e: the 8th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Ivan III conquers Novgorod
v.s: In 1472, after defeating the Novgorod Republic, Ivan III forced it to cede territory and gradually abolished its autonomy. Novgorod was the richest city-state of Russia; its fall was a key step in Moscow's unification of Russia.

v.t: Portuguese cross the equator
v.s: In 1472, Portuguese navigator Lopes Gonçalves sailed across the equator into the Southern Hemisphere. He observed that the southern sky was different from the north, providing crucial knowledge for later navigators.

d.e: the 9th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Rise of the Safavid order in Persia
v.s: In 1473, the Safavid order was growing in northern Persia. It was a Shi'ite Muslim order founded by Safi-ad-din. Its leader Ismail I would later establish the Safavid Empire (1501), making Shi'ism the state religion of Persia.

v.t: Ming and Tatars reach a peace agreement
v.s: In 1473 (the 9th year of Chenghua), the Chenghua Emperor and the Tatars reached a short-lived peace agreement. The Tatar khan Batu Möngke (Dayan Khan) was rising in the Mongol steppe. The Ming set up the "Nine Border Garrisons" along the frontier.

d.e: the 10th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Isabella ascends the throne of Castile
v.s: On December 11, 1474, King Henry IV of Castile died. His half-sister Isabella declared herself queen, but faced opposition from King Afonso V of Portugal (who was engaged to Henry's daughter Joanna). Isabella went to war with Portugal and eventually won in 1479, securing her throne.

v.t: Ming builds the Ming tombs (Shisanling)
v.s: In 1474, the Ming began building imperial tombs at Tianshou Mountain in Changping, Beijing – the later Ming Shisanling (Thirteen Tombs). The first built was the Yuling (tomb of the Tianshun Emperor). Most subsequent Ming emperors were buried there.

d.e: the 11th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Michelangelo born
v.s: On March 6, 1475, Michelangelo Buonarroti was born in Caprese, near Florence. He was one of the three giants of the Renaissance, excelling in sculpture, painting, architecture, and poetry. His masterpieces include David, the Sistine Chapel ceiling, The Last Judgment, and the dome of St. Peter's Basilica.

v.t: Burgundian Wars – prelude to the Battle of Nancy
v.s: In 1475, conflict intensified between Duke Charles the Bold of Burgundy and the Swiss Confederacy. He attempted to subdue the Swiss, but Swiss pikemen repeatedly defeated his armies. Charles the Bold was later killed at the Battle of Nancy in 1477.

v.t: Ming establishes Yucai Tang
v.s: In 1475 (the 11th year of Chenghua), the Ming established the Yucai Tang (perhaps an educational institution) in the capital to train various types of official talent, part of the Ming official education system.

d.e: the 12th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Sforza rule in Milan
v.s: In 1476, Duke Galeazzo Maria Sforza of Milan was assassinated. His son Gian Galeazzo, a child, succeeded, with his uncle Ludovico Sforza acting as regent. Ludovico later became the de facto ruler of Milan and patronized Leonardo da Vinci.

v.t: Ming conflict with the Jianzhou Jurchens
v.s: In 1476 (the 12th year of Chenghua), the Ming and the Jianzhou Jurchens clashed militarily. The Ming sent Zhao Fu to attack the Jurchen tribes. The Ming's scorched-earth policy inflamed the conflict and failed to solve the Jurchen problem fundamentally.

d.e: the 13th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Charles the Bold dies – fall of the Duchy of Burgundy
v.s: On January 5, 1477, Charles the Bold was killed at the Battle of Nancy, fighting against a combined Swiss-Lorraine army. His body was later recovered from a frozen river. With no male heir, the Duchy of Burgundy was absorbed by King Louis XI of France, while its Low Countries territories passed through his daughter Mary to the Habsburg family. The fall of Burgundy reshaped Western European politics.

v.t: Ming abolishes the Western Depot
v.s: In 1477 (the 13th year of Chenghua), the Chenghua Emperor abolished the Western Depot following opposition from officials. The Western Depot, a special secret police agency even more powerful than the Eastern Depot, had been headed by the eunuch Wang Zhi. Its brutal rule caused widespread revulsion in the court and country.

d.e: the 14th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Spanish Inquisition established
v.s: On November 1, 1478, the Spanish monarchs Ferdinand II and Isabella I obtained papal permission from Pope Sixtus IV to establish the Spanish Inquisition. The first Grand Inquisitor was Tomás de Torquemada. The Inquisition ruthlessly persecuted heretics, focusing mainly on Jews and Muslims who had converted to Christianity. Over more than three centuries, the Inquisition became one of the harshest religious tribunals in Europe.

v.t: Pazzi Conspiracy – assassination attempt on Lorenzo de' Medici
v.s: On April 26, 1478, the Pazzi family, rivals of the Medici, attempted to assassinate Lorenzo and his brother Giuliano in Florence Cathedral. Giuliano was killed, but Lorenzo survived, wounded. Lorenzo then brutally retaliated, executing the conspirators and consolidating Medici rule.

d.e: the 15th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Unification of Spain – Aragon and Castile merge
v.s: In 1479, Ferdinand II inherited the throne of Aragon, and he and his wife Isabella I jointly ruled Castile and Aragon, forming the Kingdom of Spain. Each kingdom retained its own laws and parliaments, but foreign and military affairs were unified. The unification enabled the completion of the Reconquista and the sponsorship of Columbus's voyage to the New World.

v.t: Ming campaigns against the Jianzhou Jurchens
v.s: In 1479, the Chenghua Emperor sent the eunuch Wang Zhi with troops against the Jianzhou Jurchens, destroying many Jurchen fortifications and granaries. However, the Jurchens soon recovered and continued to resist.

d.e: the 16th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Grand Duchy of Moscow defeats the Golden Horde – Great Stand on the Ugra River
v.s: In 1480, Khan Akhmat of the Golden Horde marched against Moscow. Ivan III confronted the Mongols on the Ugra River. Due to severe cold, supply difficulties, and the failure of his Polish ally to appear, Akhmat withdrew. Moscow was thus completely free of the Mongol "Tatar Yoke," achieving independence. This was a major turning point in Russian history.

v.t: Portugal colonizes the Congo
v.s: In 1480, Portugal established diplomatic relations with the Kingdom of Kongo. The Kongo king Nzinga Nkuwu (João I) converted to Christianity, and Portuguese missionaries entered Kongo. But the Portuguese also began the slave trade, which ultimately destroyed Kongolese society.

v.t: Ming briefly restores sea transport
v.s: In 1480, the Chenghua Emperor briefly restored sea transport to relieve food shortages in the north, but soon stopped it again due to official opposition. The Ming long relied on the Grand Canal; the maritime ban severely hindered overseas trade.

d.e: the 17th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II dies
v.s: On May 3, 1481, Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II died at age 49. He had conquered Constantinople, taken Serbia, Bosnia, and Albania, and fought Venice, earning the title "the Conqueror." His son Bayezid II succeeded him, and the empire briefly experienced a succession struggle.

v.t: Revolt of the Bozhou chieftain in Ming
v.s: In 1481 (the 17th year of Chenghua), Yang You, the chieftain (xuanweishi) of Bozhou (present-day Zunyi, Sichuan), rebelled due to conflicts with Ming officials. The Ming sent troops to suppress the revolt. After Yang You surrendered, he was transferred to a new post. The Yang chieftain clan had ruled Bozhou since the Tang dynasty and was finally pacified in the Wanli era.

d.e: the 18th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Portuguese reach the Congo River mouth along the West African coast
v.s: In 1482, Portuguese navigator Diogo Cão sailed down the West African coast, reached the mouth of the Congo River, and erected a stone pillar there as a marker. He continued south to the Angolan coast, establishing direct contact with the Kingdom of Kongo.

v.t: Ming bans luxury goods like jewels
v.s: In 1482, due to fiscal difficulties, the Chenghua Emperor banned the trade of jewels, pearls, and other luxury goods, and reduced court expenses, reflecting the financial strain of the mid-Ming.

d.e: the 19th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Martin Luther born
v.s: On November 10, 1483, Martin Luther was born in Eisleben, Holy Roman Empire (present-day Germany). He later became the initiator of the Protestant Reformation, posting the Ninety-five Theses in 1517 and founding the Lutheran church, transforming European religion and politics.

v.t: Raphael born
v.s: On March 28, 1483, Raffaello Sanzio (Raphael) was born in Urbino, Italy. One of the three great Renaissance masters, his works are known for their grace and harmony, including The School of Athens and the Sistine Madonna.

v.t: Ming briefly revives and then abolishes the Western Depot
v.s: In 1483 (the 19th year of Chenghua), the Chenghua Emperor briefly restored the Western Depot but then abolished it again due to official opposition. The emperor's inconsistency regarding the secret police reflected the political chaos of the Chenghua era.

d.e: the 20th year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Portuguese envoys reach the Kingdom of Kongo
v.s: In 1484, King John II of Portugal sent envoys to the Kingdom of Kongo. The Kongo king Nzinga Nkuwu agreed to send a return mission to Portugal and accepted Christian missionaries. Relations became close, but later fell victim to the slave trade.

v.t: Ming builds the Shanhaiguan section of the Great Wall
v.s: In 1484 (the 20th year of Chenghua), the Ming undertook large-scale construction of the Great Wall section at Shanhaiguan (Qinhuangdao, Hebei). Shanhaiguan, situated at the strategic neck of the Liaoxi Corridor, was a vital pass against Mongols and Jurchens. It was further expanded in the Jiajing and Wanli eras, acquiring the majestic scale of the "First Pass Under Heaven."

d.e: the 21st year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Battle of Bosworth Field – establishment of the Tudor dynasty
v.s: On August 22, 1485, Henry Tudor, a distant Lancastrian claimant, defeated King Richard III of York at Bosworth Field. Richard III was killed, ending the Wars of the Roses. Henry Tudor ascended as Henry VII, founding the Tudor dynasty. He united the red and white roses by marrying Elizabeth of York, ushering in a new era in English history.

v.t: Ming investigates salt administration corruption
v.s: In 1485 (the 21st year of Chenghua), the Ming court launched a nationwide investigation into corruption in the salt administration. Salt was a vital source of Ming revenue. Local officials and salt merchants colluded, leading to rampant private salt sales and accumulation of official salt. The emperor sent multiple teams to audit salt certificates and taxes.

d.e: the 22nd year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Maximilian I elected Holy Roman Emperor
v.s: In 1486, Maximilian I was elected King of the Romans; he became Holy Roman Emperor in 1493. He was the architect of the Habsburg dynasty, securing through marriage and inheritance the Burgundian lands, Spain and its colonies, laying the foundation for the vast empire of his grandson Charles V.

v.t: Yellow River floods in Ming
v.s: In 1486, the Yellow River breached its dikes at Kaifeng, inundating vast farmland and villages, leaving hundreds of thousands homeless. The Ming organized relief efforts, but flood control projects cost enormous sums.

d.e: the 23rd year of the Chenghua era

v.t: Dias rounds the Cape of Good Hope
v.s: In August 1487, Portuguese navigator Bartolomeu Dias, commissioned by King John II, led a fleet of two caravels and a supply ship down the West African coast to find a sea route to India. In March 1488, a storm blew the fleet eastward; when they sighted land, they realized they had rounded the southern tip of Africa. Dias named it the Cape of Storms, but the king later renamed it the Cape of Good Hope. This paved the way for Vasco da Gama's voyage to India a decade later.

v.t: Chenghua Emperor dies; Hongzhi Emperor ascends
v.s: On September 9, 1487, the Chenghua Emperor died at age 41. His son Zhu Youtang ascended as the Hongzhi Emperor, with the era name Hongzhi. The Hongzhi Emperor was diligent and benevolent, employing worthy ministers, and his reign is known as the "Hongzhi Restoration." He was monogamous, having only one empress, Empress Zhang – the only emperor in Chinese history to practice monogamy.

d.e: the 1st year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Hongzhi Emperor ascends; beginning of the Hongzhi Restoration
v.s: In 1488, the Hongzhi Emperor formally adopted the era name Hongzhi.
