He swept away the abuses of the Chenghua court, dismissed corrupt eunuchs and officials, appointed worthy ministers like Liu Jian, Xie Qian, and Li Dongyang, reformed the salt and grain transport systems, reduced taxes, and revived the dynasty's prosperity. He was diligent in government, holding morning and afternoon court sessions, and is considered one of the most able and virtuous emperors of the Ming.

v.t: Dias returns to Lisbon
v.s: In December 1488, Dias returned to Lisbon and reported to King John II that he had rounded the Cape of Good Hope. Although he had not reached India, he had proven that a sea route from the Atlantic to the Indian Ocean was possible.

d.e: the 2nd year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Cyprus becomes a Venetian colony
v.s: In 1489, the Republic of Venice forced Queen Catherine Cornaro of Cyprus to abdicate and formally annexed Cyprus as a colony. Cyprus became a vital Venetian naval base and trading post in the eastern Mediterranean until it was conquered by the Ottomans in 1571.

v.t: Ming audits land concealment
v.s: In 1489, the Hongzhi Emperor ordered a nationwide audit of land to crack down on powerful families hiding land to evade taxes. However, local officials were complicit, and the audit had limited effect.

d.e: the 3rd year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Leonardo da Vinci's scientific notebooks and anatomical studies
v.s: Around 1490, Leonardo da Vinci began systematically recording his scientific notebooks, covering anatomy, hydraulics, optics, mechanics, and more. He dissected dozens of human bodies, drawing precise anatomical plates, and designed many advanced mechanical devices (e.g., flying machines, submarines, tanks). These notebooks reflect his extraordinary creativity as a scientist.

v.t: Prelude to the establishment of the Safavid dynasty in Persia
v.s: In 1490, Safavid order leader Haydar was killed in battle, and his young son Ismail was forced into hiding. Ismail would later establish the Safavid Empire in 1501, unifying Persia.

d.e: the 4th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Rise of the Askia dynasty in the Songhai Empire
v.s: In 1491, Songhai ruler Sonni Ali died, triggering civil war. The general Muhammad Ture (Askia Muhammad) seized power in 1493, founding the Askia dynasty. During his reign (1493-1528), he brought Songhai to its height, making it the largest empire in West Africa.

v.t: Ming issues the Wenxing Tiaoli
v.s: In 1491, the Hongzhi Emperor promulgated the Wenxing Tiaoli (Articles on Punishment), a supplement to the Great Ming Code addressing new issues in legal practice. It reflected the development of mid-Ming law.

d.e: the 5th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Columbus discovers the Americas
v.s: On April 17, 1492, Queen Isabella I of Spain signed the Capitulations of Santa Fe with the Genoese navigator Christopher Columbus, funding his westward voyage to find a route to India and China. On August 3, Columbus set sail from Palos with three ships: the Santa María, the Pinta, and the Niña. On the morning of October 12, lookout Rodrigo de Triana sighted land on an island in the Bahamas (San Salvador). Columbus believed he had reached the East Indies and called the natives "Indians." He brought back gold, spices, and captives, opening sustained European contact with the Americas.

v.t: Fall of Granada – completion of the Reconquista
v.s: On January 2, 1492, Spanish forces captured Granada, the last Muslim stronghold on the Iberian Peninsula. The Nasrid sultan Boabdil handed over the keys of the city to Ferdinand and Isabella. After nearly 800 years, the Reconquista was complete, and a unified Spain emerged as a European power. On March 31, 1492, the Alhambra Decree was issued, expelling Jews who refused to convert.

v.t: Beginning of the Columbian Exchange
v.s: After Columbus reached the Americas, an unprecedented biological exchange began between the Old and New Worlds. American crops such as maize, potatoes, sweet potatoes, tomatoes, chili peppers, peanuts, and tobacco were introduced to the Old World, profoundly changing global agriculture and diets. Old World wheat, rice, sugar cane, coffee, horses, cattle, sheep, and diseases like smallpox and measles devastated the native populations of the Americas, reducing them by over 90%. The Columbian Exchange was the most significant ecological event of the past 10,000 years.

d.e: the 6th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Papal line of demarcation (Treaty of Tordesillas precursor)
v.s: On May 4, 1493, Pope Alexander VI issued a bull drawing a line 100 leagues west of the Azores, giving Spain all lands west of the line and Portugal all lands east. This was to mediate disputes between Spain and Portugal over newly discovered lands. The following year, the two countries signed the Treaty of Tordesillas, moving the line 370 leagues west of Cape Verde.

v.t: Ming bans private minting of copper coins
v.s: In 1493 (the 6th year of Hongzhi), the Hongzhi Emperor reaffirmed the ban on private minting of copper coins. The Ming suffered from a chronic shortage of copper and currency, and rampant private minting led to inflation. Although the court repeatedly issued orders, they were largely ineffective.

d.e: the 7th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: French invasion of Italy – outbreak of the Italian Wars
v.s: In August 1494, King Charles VIII of France invaded Italy with about 30,000 troops, claiming the Neapolitan throne. His army carried many cannon, quickly swept down the peninsula, and captured Naples in February 1495. This initiated the Italian Wars (1494-1559), primarily involving France, Spain, the Holy Roman Empire, and the Papal States. Italy became a battleground for the great powers, and the Italian city-states declined.

v.t: Treaty of Tordesillas signed
v.s: On June 7, 1494, Spain and Portugal, mediated by the papacy, signed the Treaty of Tordesillas, fixing the line of demarcation 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde islands (about 46° W longitude). The treaty granted Portugal all lands east and Spain all lands west, essentially dividing the non-European world into two colonial empires. Brazil later became Portuguese because of this treaty.

d.e: the 8th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Imperial reforms in the Holy Roman Empire
v.s: In 1495, Emperor Maximilian I pushed through reforms at the Diet of Worms: declaring an Eternal Public Peace, banning private feuds; establishing an Imperial Chamber Court; dividing the empire into circles; and levying a common tax (Common Penny). The reforms aimed at centralization, but were resisted by the princes, and the empire remained a loose federation.

v.t: Ming builds the Porcelain Tower of Nanjing
v.s: In 1495, the Hongzhi Emperor ordered the construction of the Porcelain Tower (Bao'en Temple) in Nanjing to honor his parents. The tower stood about 78 meters high, glazed in white porcelain, and was considered one of the "Seven Wonders of the Medieval World." It was destroyed during the Taiping Rebellion in 1856.

d.e: the 9th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Spanish-English marriage alliance
v.s: In 1496, the Spanish crown prince John (son of Ferdinand and Isabella) married Margaret, daughter of Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I; however John died the next year. Another important marriage was in 1501, when the Spanish princess Catherine of Aragon married Arthur, Prince of Wales; after Arthur's death, she married Henry VIII. These alliances strengthened Habsburg control over Europe.

v.t: Ming bans private tea trade
v.s: In 1496, the Ming reaffirmed the ban on private tea trade to Tibet and Mongolia through the tea-horse exchange system. Tea-horse trade was an important means of controlling the frontier; private tea smuggling undermined national defense.

d.e: the 10th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Da Gama opens the sea route to India
v.s: On July 8, 1497, Portuguese navigator Vasco da Gama, commissioned by King Manuel I, set sail from Lisbon with four ships and about 170 men, seeking a sea route to India around Africa. He followed Dias's route, rounded the Cape of Good Hope on November 22, reached Malindi on the East African coast in April of the following year, and with the help of an Arab pilot, Ahmad ibn Majid, crossed the Indian Ocean, arriving at Calicut (Kozhikode) on May 20, 1498. Da Gama's voyage broke the Arab and Venetian monopoly on the spice trade, establishing a direct sea route from Europe to India.

v.t: John Cabot discovers Newfoundland
v.s: In May 1497, the Italian navigator John Cabot (Giovanni Caboto), commissioned by King Henry VII of England, sailed from Bristol on the 50-ton Matthew, heading west across the Atlantic. On June 24, he sighted land, likely Newfoundland (or Cape Breton Island), believing it to be Asia. He discovered the rich cod fisheries of North America, providing a basis for later English colonization.

v.t: Leonardo da Vinci completes The Last Supper
v.s: In 1497 (around 1498), Leonardo da Vinci completed the mural The Last Supper on the refectory wall of the Convent of Santa Maria delle Grazie in Milan. He used innovative perspective and vividly captured the moment Jesus announced, "One of you will betray me," showing the reactions of the twelve apostles. Due to experimental egg tempera technique, the painting began to deteriorate soon after completion and has undergone numerous restorations. It became an iconic masterpiece of Renaissance art.

d.e: the 11th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Da Gama reaches India
v.s: On May 20, 1498, Vasco da Gama's fleet arrived at Calicut, India. The local ruler, the Zamorin, was unimpressed by the gifts da Gama brought, and trade was difficult. Da Gama departed on August 29, leaving a few Portuguese to establish a trading post. He returned to Lisbon in September 1499 with a cargo of pepper, cinnamon, and gems, earning a profit of 60 times the voyage's cost. This route enabled Portugal to bypass the Ottoman and Venetian monopolies and dominate the European spice trade.

v.t: Columbus's third voyage discovers the South American mainland
v.s: On May 30, 1498, Columbus set sail with six ships on his third voyage. On July 31, he reached Trinidad and entered the Gulf of Paria, sighting the South American mainland (present-day Venezuela). He realized it was a new continent but still believed it was part of Asia. Mismanagement of the colonies led the Spanish crown to send a commissioner who arrested Columbus and sent him back in chains, though he was soon released.

v.t: Ming compiles the Da Ming Huidian
v.s: In 1498, the Hongzhi Emperor ordered Li Dongyang and others to compile the Da Ming Huidian (Collected Statutes of the Ming Dynasty), a compendium of Ming institutions and regulations. It was completed in 1502 and printed in 1509, an important source for the study of Ming governance.

d.e: the 12th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Swiss independence – Treaty of Basel
v.s: In 1499, Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian I tried to incorporate the Swiss Confederacy into the empire's circle system, triggering the Swabian War. The Swiss mountain infantry repeatedly defeated the imperial armies. On September 22, the Treaty of Basel recognized Swiss de facto independence, and the Swiss Confederacy left the Holy Roman Empire.

v.t: Ming reconstructs the Confucian Temple at Qufu
v.s: In 1499 (the 12th year of Hongzhi), the Hongzhi Emperor ordered a large-scale reconstruction of the Confucian Temple at Qufu, Shandong. The temple is a shrine to Confucius. The Hongzhi-era reconstruction further expanded its scale.

d.e: the 13th year of the Hongzhi era

v.t: Portuguese navigator Cabral discovers Brazil
v.s: On April 22, 1500, King Manuel I of Portugal sent Pedro Álvares Cabral with a fleet to India. To avoid calms off Africa, they sailed far west and unexpectedly reached the coast of South America (near present-day Porto Seguro, Brazil). Cabral claimed the land for Portugal, naming it Vera Cruz. By the Treaty of Tordesillas, Brazil fell on the Portuguese side, becoming Portugal's only South American colony.

v.t: Ottoman-Venetian war ends
v.s: In 1500, Ottoman Sultan Bayezid II and the Republic of Venice signed a peace treaty: Venice recognized Ottoman conquests in Greece and Albania, retaining only some Ionian islands and part of Crete. Venetian dominance in the eastern Mediterranean further declined.

v.t: Flourishing commerce and rise of market towns in the Jiangnan region of Ming
v.s: Around 1500, the Jiangnan region of the Ming dynasty experienced remarkable commercial and industrial growth. Cities such as Suzhou, Songjiang, and Hangzhou became centers of silk and cotton textile production, with specialized towns like Shengze and Nanxun emerging. The sprouts of capitalism appeared, but were constrained by the maritime ban and the policy of emphasizing agriculture over commerce.

v.t: On the eve of the Safavid Empire
v.s: In 1500, the young Ismail I gathered followers in northeastern Persia and rose against the Aq Qoyunlu. In 1501, he captured Tabriz, proclaimed himself Shah, and established the Safavid Empire, making Twelver Shi'ism the state religion – profoundly changing Persian history.

v.t: Lapita culture: ancestors of the Polynesians
v.s: From about 1500 to 500 BCE, the Lapita culture emerged in the Bismarck Archipelago (present-day Papua New Guinea) and rapidly expanded into the southwestern Pacific. The Lapita people are known for their distinctive dentate-stamped pottery; they were the first to settle Fiji, Tonga, and Samoa, and are the direct ancestors of modern Polynesians, Micronesians, and some Melanesians. The Lapita were among history's greatest seafarers, crossing thousands of kilometers of the Pacific in just a few centuries.
